intro: The most concrete split between Maze and Hotjar is the pricing model: Maze runs on freemium, while Hotjar uses subscription pricing, with a free tier. That shapes how predictable your monthly spend is and how much of the cost sits in infrastructure versus per-seat or per-event fees. Beyond the headline, Maze carries medium lock-in (research data exportable) and freemium; Hotjar carries low lock-in (low — simple snippet, easy to replace) and subscription pricing, with a free tier. Maze is a fit when you are ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch. Hotjar is a fit when you are ux debugging and optimization. The honest trade-off: Maze is explicitly not for engineering teams looking for production session replay and error triage; Hotjar is explicitly not for apps with strict privacy requirements (healthcare). On the product side, Maze is described as User research and testing platform for rapid prototype testing, card sorting, tree testing, surveys and usability analytics with session recordings, and Hotjar is described as UX analytics platform combining heatmaps, session recordings, surveys, and funnel analysis for web products.
quick_take: Maze is for ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch; Hotjar is for ux debugging and optimization; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Maze if its freemium pricing model fits your budget shape better than Hotjar's subscription model, and you are ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch.
verdict_b: Choose Hotjar if its subscription pricing model fits your budget shape better than Maze's freemium model, and you are ux debugging and optimization.

faq.a: Both tools use freemium pricing. Maze is listed as freemium; Hotjar as subscription pricing, with a free tier. Without specific volume and seat assumptions it is not possible to say which is cheaper in general.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Maze to Hotjar?
faq.a: Migration from Maze to Hotjar is feasible but not trivial: Maze has medium lock-in (research data exportable). Core data can move; integrations and workflow logic need rebuild. Allow for a parallel-run period.

On our scoring Hotjar rates 4/5 for developer experience versus 3/5 for Maze. That suggests Hotjar feels more polished for everyday developer tasks. We still recommend a short hands-on trial on your stack before standardising.

faq.q: Is Hotjar a good alternative to Maze?
faq.a: Hotjar is pitched at ux debugging and optimization, which overlaps with Maze's fit (ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch) but is not identical. Note that Hotjar is explicitly not for apps with strict privacy requirements (healthcare), so it only replaces Maze if you are outside that exclusion. The lock-in profile differs (medium vs low), which is the main thing to weigh.
